text: The surgeon performs a robotic distal pancreatectomy, beginning by opening the lesser sac and mobilizing the stomach. The gastrocolic omentum is opened, and the short gastric vessels are taken down, revealing a large tumor in the proximal body of the pancreas. The splenic flexure is visible, and the spleen is exposed as the short gastric vessels are fully dissected.

The surgeon removes adhesions overlying the pancreatic tumor and sutures the stomach to the abdominal wall for retraction, providing clear exposure of the pancreas. An ultrasound is used to identify the location of nearby vessels.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas away from the transverse colon and splenic flexure, isolating the pancreas-spleen complex. The splenic flexure is difficult to mobilize, extending across the spleen. The surgeon works laterally to medially to mobilize the splenic flexure, assisted by a colorectal surgeon. The redundant splenic flexure is fully mobilized with the omentum, revealing the pancreas and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) joining. The surgeon begins searching for the splenic vein.

The surgeon isolates the splenic vein and splenic artery, dividing them before dividing the pancreas. Using a combination of hook Maryland and vessel sealer, the splenic vein is visible with branches extending to the pancreas. The surgeon carefully isolates the splenic artery to avoid injuring the nearby portal vein, while the bulky tumor limits exposure to the underlying splenic artery.

The surgeon attempts to separate the pancreatic neck from the hepatic artery, aiming to expose the portal vein. The pancreas is divided using a blue load stapler, revealing the underlying portal vein. The body and tail of the pancreas are mobilized to the left, aiding in the exposure of the splenic artery's origin. The splenic vein is visible, lifted with the third arm. The surgeon clips and staples the pancreas to prevent bleeding and pseudoaneurysm formation.

The surgeon uses a white load stapler to divide the vein, followed by mobilizing the pancreas and spleen from the retroperitoneum. The tumor, identified as a five-centimeter solid pseudopapillary neoplasm, is visible. The surgical field shows negative margins and twelve lymph nodes tested negative. The surgeon places a drain, which will be tested for amylase on post-operative day three.
